flashcards.front: Who developed Extreme Programming and in what year?
flashcards.back: Kent Beck developed Extreme Programming in 1996.

flashcards.front: What are the four core values of XP?
flashcards.back: The four core values are: Communication, Simplicity, Feedback, and Courage.

flashcards.front: What is the Planning Game in XP?
flashcards.back: Planning Game is a planning practice where customers and developers collaborate to determine story points and release schedules through iterative planning.

flashcards.front: What is Pair Programming in XP?
flashcards.back: Pair Programming is a practice where two developers work together at one workstation, sharing one keyboard and screen - one writes code (driver) while the other reviews and provides feedback (navigator).
TDD is a development technique where developers write automated tests before writing the actual code, following a Red-Green-Refactor cycle.

flashcards.front: What is Continuous Integration in XP?
flashcards.back: Continuous Integration is the practice of frequently merging code changes into a shared repository, with automated builds and tests running on each integration.
Refactoring is the process of improving code structure and design without changing its external behavior, done continuously to keep code simple and maintainable.
Collective Code Ownership means any developer can modify any part of the codebase at any time, encouraging shared responsibility and preventing code bottlenecks.

flashcards.front: What is the Metaphor in XP?
flashcards.back: Metaphor is a simple shared story or description of how the system works, replacing complex technical documentation with a common language understood by all team members.

flashcards.front: What is Sustainable Pace in XP?
flashcards.back: Sustainable Pace (or 40-hour week) means team members should not work overtime consistently, ensuring long-term productivity and avoiding burnout.

flashcards.front: What are User Stories in XP?
flashcards.back: User Stories are brief descriptions of a feature from the customer's perspective, written on index cards capturing what the user needs and why.

flashcards.front: What is Simple Design in XP?
flashcards.back: Simple Design emphasizes building only what is needed now, avoiding over-engineering and complex solutions that might not be used.

flashcards.front: What are the roles in an XP team?
flashcards.back: The key roles are: Programmer, Customer, Tester, and Coach. Programmers write code, Customers define requirements, Testers verify functionality, and Coaches guide the team.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Unit Tests and Acceptance Tests in XP?
flashcards.back: Unit Tests are written by programmers to test individual code components, while Acceptance Tests are written by customers to verify that user stories work correctly from the user's perspective.

flashcards.front: What is the 'on-site customer' practice in XP?
flashcards.back: On-site Customer means a real customer or representative works with the development team daily, providing immediate feedback and clarification on requirements.
